text: Let me explore the codebase to understand how MachineConfig handles `passwd.users` and `storage.filesystems` before writing the config.

input.description: Research MachineConfig passwd.users and storage.filesystems support
input.prompt:
In the machine-config-operator repository at /home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator, I need to understand:

1. How `passwd.users` is handled by the MachineConfigDaemon — specifically, does in-place update support adding users? Look for any caveats, warnings, or "not supported" comments around passwd/user handling in the daemon code.
2. How `storage.filesystems` is handled — does in-place update support creating new filesystems? Look for any warnings or limitations.
3. Find 2-3 real example MachineConfig YAML files (in tests, docs, or examples directories) that show passwd.users or storage.filesystems usage so I can see the correct schema.
4. What Ignition spec version (e.g., 3.2, 3.4) the MCO currently targets.

Search breadth: very thorough. Focus on: pkg/daemon/, vendor/github.com/coreos/ignition, test/e2e/, and any docs/ or examples/ directories. Report file paths and line numbers for key findings.

2029	// updateFiles writes files specified by the nodeconfig to disk. it also writes
2030	// systemd units. there is no support for multiple filesystems at this point.
2031	//
2032	// in addition to files, we also write systemd units to disk. we mask, enable,
2033	// and disable unit files when appropriate. this function relies on the system
2034	// being restarted after an upgrade, so it doesn't daemon-reload or restart
2035	// any services.
2036	//
2037	// it is worth noting that this function explicitly doesn't rely on the ignition
2038	// implementation of file, unit writing, enabling or disabling. this is because
2039	// ignition is built on the assumption that it is working with a fresh system,
2040	// where as we are trying to reconcile a system that has already been running.
2041	//
2042	// in the future, this function should do any additional work to confirm that
2043	// whatever has been written is picked up by the appropriate daemons, if
2044	// required. in particular, a daemon-reload and restart for any unit files
2045	// touched.
2046	func (dn *Daemon) updateFiles(oldIgnConfig, newIgnConfig ign3types.Config, addedOrChangedUnits []ign3types.Unit, skipCertificateWrite, forceFilePresent bool) error {
2047		klog.Info("Updating files")
2048		if err := dn.writeFiles(newIgnConfig.Storage.Files, skipCertificateWrite); err != nil {
2049			return err
2050		}
2051	
2052		// With OCPBUGS-58023, we updated this flow to only write units that were either added or
2053		// updated. As can be seen in OCPBUGS-74692, this impacted the traditional method to recover
2054		// from config drifts with systemd units. It makes the `touch /run/machine-config-daemon-force`
2055		// command useless since the new flow does not rewrite all files, only the ones that have been
2056		// added or changed with the latest MC. To keep the fix for OCPBUGS-58023 and allow continue
2057		// supporting the traditional config drift recovery for systemd units, all units should be
2058		// written when a forcefile exists.
2059		unitsToWrite := addedOrChangedUnits
2060		if forceFilePresent {
2061			klog.Info("Forcefile exists, writing all units")
2062			unitsToWrite = newIgnConfig.Systemd.Units
2063		}
2064	
2065		if err := dn.writeUnits(unitsToWrite); err != nil {
2066			return err
2067		}
2068		return dn.deleteStaleData(oldIgnConfig, newIgnConfig)
2069	}

1	# MachineConfigDaemon
2	
3	## Goals
4	
5	1. Apply new machine configuration during update.
6	
7	2. Validate and verify machine's state to the requested machine configuration.
8	
9	## Non Goals
10	
11	1. MachineConfigDaemon does not execute scripts on the machines.
12	
13	## Overview
14	
15	MachineConfigDaemon is scheduled on the machines in a cluster as a DaemonSet. This daemon is responsible for performing machine updates in OpenShift 4. The update will include tasks related to the systemd units, files on disk, operating system upgrades etc. The MachineConfigDaemon updates a machine to configuration defined by MachineConfig as instructed by the MachineConfigController.
16	
17	The MachineConfigDaemon is also responsible for annotating a node with `machineconfiguration.openshift.io/ssh=accessed` when it detects an SSH access to the machine.
18	
19	## Supported vs Unsupported Ignition config changes
20	
21	The MachineConfigDaemon receives machine configuration in the form of a "rendered" or merged MachineConfig which is generated from applicable fragments by the controller.
22	
23	If the updated Ignition config contains changes compatible with the current config, the node will be updated in place.  Otherwise, it will enter a "degraded" state; the idea is that a human or automation tooling can then re-provision degraded machines.
24	
25	Not all Ignition config sections are supported; see the following table:
26	
27	Ignition spec 2 sections | Supported
28	--- | ---
29	Files | YES
30	systemd Units | YES
31	Networkd | NO
32	Users | NO *
33	Directories | NO
34	FileSystems | NO
35	Links | NO
36	Disks | NO
37	RAID | NO
38	
39	Ignition spec 3 sections | Supported
40	--- | ---
41	Files | YES
42	systemd Units | YES
43	Users | NO *
44	Groups | NO
45	Directories | NO
46	FileSystems | NO
47	Links | NO
48	Disks | NO
49	RAID | NO
50	
51	\* At this time only updates to `sshAuthorizedKeys` for user `core` are permitted. Please see [Update-SSHKeys](./Update-SSHKeys.md) for details.
52	
53	## Coordinating updates
54	
55	The MachineConfigDaemon uses [annotations defined](./MachineConfigController.md#updatecontroller-interface-with-machineconfigdaemon) on the Node object to coordinate updates with MachineConfigController for the machine.
56	
57	![MachineConfigDaemon update flow](./MachineConfigDaemonUpdate.svg)
58	
59	### States
60	
61	1. `Done` when daemon sets currentConfig = desiredConfig
62	
63	2. `Working` when daemon starts updating the machine.
64	
65	3. `Degraded` when daemon cannot continue to apply the update.
66	
67	## OS updates
68	
69	In addition to handling Ignition configs, the MachineConfigDaemon also takes
70	care of updating the base operating system.
71	
72	Updates are provided via the `OSImageURL` component of a MachineConfig object.
73	This should generally be controlled by the
74	[cluster-version-operator](https://github.com/openshift/cluster-version-operator/),
75	and its current existence in MachineConfig objects should be thought of as an
76	implementation detail.
77	
78	MachineConfigDaemon only supports updating Red Hat CoreOS, which uses rpm-ostree.
79	The `OSImageURL` refers to a container image that carries inside it an OSTree payload.  When
80	the `OSImageURL` changes, it will be passed to the [pivot](https://github.com/openshift/pivot)
81	command which is included in Red Hat CoreOS, and in turn takes care of passing it
82	to rpm-ostree.
83	
84	Once an update is prepared (in terms of a new bootloader entry which points to a
85	new OSTree "deployment" or filesystem tree), then the MachineConfigDaemon will
86	reboot.
87	
88	### Verification
89	
90	Upon start, MachineConfigDaemon queries rpm-ostree to determine the booted system version
91	and verifies it matches the expected config.
92	
93	## systemd unit updates
94	
95	MachineConfigDaemon replaces the unit service files on disk. The updated systemd services run after machine reboot.
96	
97	The daemon should prune all the systemd units that don't exist in the desiredConfig but existed before. Diff the current config and desired config, then remove the units that were removed.
98	
99	### Verification
100	
101	1. MachineConfigDaemon verifies that contents and existence of the systemd unit files.
102	
103	2. MachineConfigDaemon also verifies that the systemd service is enabled when specified in Ignition config.
104	
105	## Directory / File updates
106	
107	MachineConfigDaemon replaces the file contents on disk with the contents of the file from the desiredConfig.
108	
109	The daemon should apply any change in permissions on file / directories.
110	
111	The daemon should prune all the files and directories that don't exist in the desiredConfig but existed before. Diff the current config and desired config, then remove the nodes that were removed.
112	
113	### Verification
114	
115	When starting, MachineConfigDaemon verifies that contents and existence of the files and directories match the current configuration.  If the MachineConfigDaemon is coming up after applying a "pending" configuration, it will become current, and then verification will proceed.
116	
117	## Machine reboot
118	
119	With the exception of [rebootless updates](#rebootless-updates), the MachineConfigDaemon will drain and reboot the machine after applying the updated machine configuration.
120	
121	## Node drain
122	
123	The daemon performs a best-effort node drain before rebooting.
124	
125	The node drain behavior:
126	
127	1. Should not try to remove static pods.
128	
129	2. Should respect pod disruption policy for evictions.
130	
131	3. Should not evict pods marked with critical annotation.
132	
133	4. Should not evict itself from the node.
134	
135	### Node drain on master nodes
136	
137	The draining on master nodes should not be different from worker node as the control plane is self-hosted.
138	
139	### Node drain master in single master
140	
141	The draining of pods on the only master node will not evict the control plane as they have critical pod annotation. After rebooting the only master, the pod-checkpointer brings back the components responsible for restarting the control plane.
142	
143	### Node drain etcd static pods on masters
144	
145	Etcd is co-located on master nodes as static pods. The draining behavior defined above prevents draining of static pods to prevent interference to etcd cluster by the daemon.
146	
147	## Rebootless Updates
148	
149	As of Openshift 4.17, the MCO has added a new feature called NodeDisruptionPolicy, which allows the cluster admin to define custom behaviours during MachineConfig updates. The default policies used by NodeDisruptionPolicy reflect the behaviors mentioned below. More details about NodeDisruptionPolicy can be found [here](./NodeDisruptionPolicy.md).
150	
151	As of Openshift 4.7, the MCD gained the functionality to apply select MachineConfig updates without a full reboot flow (drain -> update -> reboot). The MCD now calculates a diff between the current and desired configurations, and it uses any changes to select one of the options listed below. For any change not listed below, or if a forcefile was set, the MCD will trigger the full reboot flow.
152	
153	The updated list of optimized updates and behaviour (as of Openshift 4.10) is as follows:
154	
155	### Without Drain
156	
157	Most reboot exceptions also skip a drain, but some have to reload crio.
158	
159	#### "None" Action
160	
161	The "None" action only performs the corresponding file write; it does not trigger a drain or a reboot. This action is taken for changes to the following items:
162	
163	1. [SSH Keys](./Update-SSHKeys.md): updated by changing `ignition.passwd.users.sshAuthorizedKeys` in a MachineConfig
164	2. kube-apiserver-to-kubelet-signer CA cert: located at `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet-ca.crt` and autorotated by the openshift-kube-apiserver operator after a 1 year expiry
165	3. [Pull Secret](./PullSecret.md): cluster-wide, located at `/var/lib/kubelet/config.json`
166	
167	#### "Reload Crio" Action
168	
169	The "Reload Crio" action performs the file write and runs a `systemctl reload crio`. It does not trigger a drain or a reboot for changes to the following items:
170	
171	1. Container signing GPG keys: these can be changed by pointing `/etc/containers/policy.json` to `/etc/machine-config-daemon/no-reboot/containers-gpg.pub` and storing keys in the latter file. Changes to either file trigger the "Reload Crio" action
172	2. Namespaced containers signing policies: these policy files located under `/etc/crio/policies` directory. Changes to files under this directory trigger the "Reload Crio" action
173	3. **Selected** `/etc/containers/registries.conf` changes: this file is generally changed via ICSP object changes. Only the following changes will avoid a drain:
174	   - addition of a registry with `pull-from-mirror=digest-only` for each mirror
175	   - addition of a mirror with `pull-from-mirror=digest-only` in a registry
176	   - appending items in the `unqualified-search-registries` list
177	
178	### With Drain
179	
180	"Reload Crio" is performed with a drain for changes to the following items:
181	
182	1. **Selected** `/etc/containers/registries.conf` changes: this file is generally changed via ICSP object changes. Node drain will take place except for changes specified [above](#Without-Drain).
183	
184	## Config Drift Detection
185	
186	### Overview
187	
188	There may be situations when the on-disk state of a node may differ or "drift"
189	from what is specified by the MachineConfig. This is usually (though not
190	always) due to a cluster admin manually editing files. In the past, the MCD
191	would only verify that the on-disk state matches what is specified by the
192	MachineConfig after a reboot. Whenever this occurs, the node and Machine Config
193	Pool (MCP) would be marked Degraded and unable to apply any MachineConfigs.
194	
195	Because reboots do not frequently occur, the config drift isn't immediately
196	detected. In cases where the config drift was intentional, the cluster admin
197	may lose the context which necessitated the config drift between the time the
198	drift occurred and when it was detected. This leads to the node and / or MCP
199	becoming degraded at an inconvenient time and can block future updates until
200	the issue is remedied.
201	
202	### Detection
203	
204	To avoid this, a Config Drift Monitor was built into the MCD in OCP 4.10. It
205	uses `fsnotify` to proactively detect config drift and mark the node `Degraded`
206	within seconds of the drift taking place.
207	
208	Whenever a filesystem write event is detected for any of the objects (Ignition
209	files and systemd units / dropins) defined in the currently applied
210	MachineConfig, the Config Drift Monitor validates that the file contents and
211	permissions fully match what the currently-applied MachineConfig specifies.
212	
213	Whenever the Config Drift Monitor detects an inconsistent object, it will:
214	1. Emit an error to the console logs.
215	1. Emit a Kubernetes event indicating that a configuration drift has occurred.
216	1. Stop further verification.
217	1. Set `machineconfiguration.openshift.io/state` to `Degraded`. 
218	
219	### Machine Config Updates
220	
221	Prior to applying a new MachineConfig, a preflight check is made to verify that
222	the current on-disk state matches the active MachineConfig. If config drift is
223	detected, `machineconfiguration.openshift.io/state` will be set to `Degraded`
224	and the update will not be applied until recovery steps are taken.
225	
226	To apply a new MachineConfig, the Config Drift Monitor is temporarily shut down.
227	This is because the config will "drift" from the current MachineConfig to the
228	new MachineConfig. Once new MachineConfig has been applied (assuming no reboot
229	is needed), the Config Drift Monitor will be restarted and supplied with the
230	newly active MachineConfig. If a reboot is required, the Config Drift Monitor
231	will be started once the MCD is out of the booting phase.
232	
233	Whenever the Config Drift Monitor is started or stopped, a Kubernetes Event
234	will be emitted. At startup, the event will also include the name of the
235	MachineConfig it is using as a reference.
236	
237	### Recovering From Config Drift
238	
239	Once config drift is detected, there are two options for recovery:
240	
241	1. Ensure that the contents and file permissons of the file on disk match what
242	the MachineConfig specifies. This can be done by manually rewriting the file
243	contents or changing the file mode. Once this is done, the MCD will reapply the
244	current MachineConfig.
245	1. Create the forcefile (`/run/machine-config-daemon-force`). This will cause
246	the MCD to bypass the preflight config checks and reapply the current
247	MachineConfig. This will also cause the node to reboot, which may not be
248	desirable.

1	# Managing SSH Keys with the MCD
2	
3	By default, the OpenShift 4 installer creates a single user named `core` (derived in spirit from CoreOS Container Linux) with optional SSH keys specified at install time.
4	
5	This controller supports updating the SSH keys of user `core` via a MachineConfig object. The SSH keys are updated for all members of the MachineConfig pool specified in the MachineConfig, for example: all worker nodes.
6	
7	Please note that RHCOS nodes will be [annotated](https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/blob/master/docs/MachineConfigDaemon.md#annotating-on-ssh-access) when accessed via SSH.
8	
9	## Unsupported Operations
10	
11	- The MCD will not add any new users.
12	
13	- The MCD will not delete the user `core`.
14	
15	- The MCD will not make any changes to any other User fields for user `core` other than `sshAuthorizedKeys`.
16	
17	## Info you will need
18	
19	You will need the following information for the MachineConfig that will be used to update your SSHKeys.
20	
21	- `machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role:` the MachineConfig that is updated will be applied to all nodes with the role specified here. For example: `master` or `worker`
22	
23	- `sshAuthorizedKeys:` you will need one or more public keys to be assigned to user `core`.  Multiple SSH Keys should begin on different lines and each be preceded by `-`.
24	
25	## Creating SSH for a pool
26	
27	In case your cluster has been installed without providing the ssh key for the `core` user at installation time (verify that `99-worker-ssh`/`99-master-ssh` are missing, by checking `oc get machineconfigs`),
28	you can easily create a MachineConfig that will ship the SSH keys to the nodes in a pool:
29	
30	```console
31	$ cat 99-worker-ssh.yaml
32	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
33	kind: MachineConfig
34	metadata:
35	  labels:
36	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
37	  name: 99-worker-ssh
38	spec:
39	  config:
40	    ignition:
41	      version: 2.2.0
42	    passwd:
43	      users:
44	      - name: core
45	        sshAuthorizedKeys:
46	        - ssh-rsa ABC123....
47	        - ssh-ed25519 XYZ7890....
48	```
49	
50	Then just create the file with `oc`:
51	
52	```console
53	$ oc create -f 99-worker-ssh.yaml
54	```
55	
56	The MCO will take care of shipping the SSH keys to the nodes in the `worker` pool. The same procedure applies to any other pool in the cluster.
57	
58	## Updating SSH for workers
59	
60	List all the machineconfigs currently in the cluster.
61	
62	```console
63	$ oc get machineconfigs
64	NAME                                               GENERATEDBYCONTROLLER               IGNITIONVERSION   CREATED
65	00-master                                          4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
66	00-worker                                          4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
67	01-master-container-runtime                        4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
68	01-master-kubelet                                  4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
69	01-worker-container-runtime                        4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m39s
70	01-worker-kubelet                                  4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m39s
71	99-master-ssh                                                                          2.2.0             3m57s
72	99-worker-ssh                                                                          2.2.0             3m56s
73	rendered-master-753f1b4476475611360ba65b4d0d378d   4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m36s
74	rendered-worker-6a77758776c24585f8db809b64569b6f   4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m36s
75	```
76	
77	`99-worker-ssh` contains the current SSHAuthorizedKeys for `worker` pool.
78	
79	```console
80	$ oc get machineconfigs 99-worker-ssh -oyaml
81	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
82	kind: MachineConfig
83	metadata:
84	  labels:
85	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
86	  name: 99-worker-ssh
87	spec:
88	  config:
89	    ignition:
90	      version: 2.2.0
91	    passwd:
92	      users:
93	      - name: core
94	        sshAuthorizedKeys:
95	        - ssh-rsa ABC123....
96	        - ssh-ed25519 XYZ7890....
97	        - ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 AAAAE2....
98	```
99	
100	Export the `99-worker-ssh` to edit the `SSHAuthorizedKeys`.
101	
102	```sh
103	oc get machineconfigs 99-worker-ssh -oyaml > update-ssh-worker.yaml
104	```
105	
106	Update the `sshAuthorizedKeys` for `core` user in `update-ssh-worker.yaml`.
107	
108	```yaml
109	# update-ssh-worker.yaml
110	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
111	kind: MachineConfig
112	metadata:
113	  labels:
114	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker

121	      users:
122	      - name: core
123	        sshAuthorizedKeys:
124	        - ssh rsa ABC123....
125	        - ssh rsa XYZ7890....
126	```
127	
128	Now with your MachineConfig yaml file (using the example above), apply the changes.
129	
130	```sh
131	oc apply -f update-ssh-worker.yaml
132	```
133	
134	You should see the new MachineConfig being generated by the controller to roll the changes:
135	
136	```console
137	$ oc get machineconfigs
138	NAME                                               GENERATEDBYCONTROLLER               IGNITIONVERSION   CREATED
139	00-master                                          4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
140	00-worker                                          4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
141	01-master-container-runtime                        4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
142	01-master-kubelet                                  4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m40s
143	01-worker-container-runtime                        4.0.0-alpha.0-116-gdc9b354d-dirty   2.2.0             3m39s

145	99-master-ssh                                                                          2.2.0             3m57s

152	You are then able to monitor the MCD logs of a worker or master (whichever the config applied to), which should check the proposed changes and reboot into the new config:
153

158	If the update was succesfully applied, you should expect to see lines similar to in these logs:

76	// NewMachineConfigExtended returns a more comprehensive machine config
77	func NewMachineConfigExtended(
78		name string,
79		labels map[string]string,
80		annotations map[string]string,
81		files []ign3types.File,
82		units []ign3types.Unit,

91		}
92		if annotations == nil {
93			annotations = map[string]string{}
94		}
95	
96		ignConfig := &ign3types.Config{
97			Ignition: ign3types.Ignition{
98				Version: ign3types.MaxVersion.String(),
99			},
100			Storage: ign3types.Storage{
101				Files: files,
102			},
103			Systemd: ign3types.Systemd{
104				Units: units,
105			},

/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/vendor/github.com/coreos/ignition/v2/config/v3_5/types/passwd.go:17:func (p PasswdUser) Key() string {
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/vendor/github.com/coreos/ignition/v2/config/v3_5/types/passwd.go:21:func (g PasswdGroup) Key() string {

Here is the complete, thorough report on all four questions.

---

## 1. `passwd.users` — In-Place Update Support and Caveats

### What is supported

In-place updates to `passwd.users` are strictly limited. The `isConfigReconcilable` function in `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/common/reconcile.go` (lines 101–109) gates all passwd changes through `validatePasswdChanges`.

`validatePasswdChanges` (lines 196–219) enforces three rules:

1. **No changes to `Passwd.Groups` are permitted** (line 197).
2. **Only the user named `"core"` is permitted in `Passwd.Users`** (lines 204–207). Any non-core user causes an immediate reconcilable error: `"ignition passwd user section contains unsupported changes: non-core user"`.
3. **For the `core` user, only `SSHAuthorizedKeys` and `PasswordHash` fields may be set** — all other fields on `PasswdUser` must be empty. This is enforced by `verifyUserFields` (lines 177–192), which blanks those two fields and does a `reflect.DeepEqual` against an empty struct. If anything else (e.g., `HomeDir`, `Shell`, `Groups`) is non-zero, it returns: `"SSH keys and password hash are not reconcilable"`.

The official documentation at `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfigDaemon.md` (lines 43–51) states plainly:

> Users | NO \*
> \* At this time only updates to `sshAuthorizedKeys` for user `core` are permitted.

And `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/Update-SSHKeys.md` (lines 9–15) says:

> - The MCD will not add any new users.
> - The MCD will not delete the user `core`.
> - The MCD will not make any changes to any other User fields for user `core` other than `sshAuthorizedKeys`.

Note that `passwordHash` is also handled at runtime (see `SetPasswordHash` in `update.go` lines 2572–2614), and the `verifyUserFields` function does permit it alongside `SSHAuthorizedKeys`. So `passwordHash` for user `core` is in practice also updated in-place, even though the docs only mention SSH keys.

### Critical caveat: adding users is explicitly blocked

`updateSSHKeys` and `SetPasswordHash` in `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/daemon/update.go` both contain this guard (lines 2582–2586 and 2648–2651):

```go
case errors.As(err, &uErr):
    klog.Info("core user does not exist, and creating users is not supported, so ignoring configuration specified for core user")
    return nil
```

The reconcile check at the render-controller layer already blocks non-core users before they even reach the daemon. So:

- **Adding new users via `passwd.users` is completely unsupported** — it will be rejected at reconcile time as irreconcilable.
- **Changing fields other than `sshAuthorizedKeys` / `passwordHash` for `core` is unsupported** — same rejection path.
- **Changes to `passwd.groups` are unsupported**.

The `machineConfigDiff` struct (line 1499) tracks `passwd bool`, and it is set (line 1562) any time `oldIgn.Passwd != newIgn.Passwd`. When set, only SSH key and password hash operations are performed (lines 1222–1266). There is no code path in `update.go` that calls `useradd` or otherwise creates a user.

---

## 2. `storage.filesystems` — In-Place Update Support and Caveats

### Filesystems are irreconcilable by default

The `isConfigReconcilable` function at lines 129–132 of `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/common/reconcile.go`:

```go
if !slices.Contains(storageOverrides, opv1.IrreconcilableValidationOverridesStorageFileSystems) &&
    !reflect.DeepEqual(oldIgn.Storage.Filesystems, newIgn.Storage.Filesystems) {
    return fmt.Errorf("ignition filesystems section contains changes")
}
```

Any change to `storage.filesystems` between the old and new rendered config is rejected as irreconcilable unless a special override is present (see below). The same applies to `storage.disks` and `storage.raid`.

The `updateFiles` function comment at line 2030 in `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/daemon/update.go` says:

```go
// systemd units. there is no support for multiple filesystems at this point.
```

And `docs/MachineConfigDaemon.md` confirms (lines 34 and 46):

> FileSystems | NO (for both Ignition spec 2 and spec 3)

The `machineConfigDiff` struct does not even contain a `filesystems` field — the daemon does not track or act on filesystem changes in any way during in-place updates.

### The `IrreconcilableValidationOverrides` escape hatch

There is a newer mechanism, `IrreconcilableValidationOverrides`, which can suppress the reconcilable check for `storage.filesystems` (and `storage.disks`, `storage.raid`). When enabled, the check is bypassed and the new config is accepted, but the MCD itself still does not apply the filesystem changes to the running node — it just doesn't reject them. The `irreconcilableDifferencesReportGeneratorImpl` in `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/daemon/irreconcilable_diff_reporter.go` (line 151) then reports the filesystem delta to `MachineConfigNode.status.irreconcilableChanges` for observability. The intended use case is for newly provisioned nodes to pick up the disk/filesystem config at install time.

The e2e test in `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/test/extended-priv/mco_storage.go` (lines 602–609) demonstrates this: existing nodes get `spec.config.storage.filesystems` reported as irreconcilable, while newly provisioned nodes with the right hardware will boot clean.

## 3. Real Example MachineConfig YAML Files

### Example 1: `passwd.users` with SSH keys (bootstrap testdata)

`/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/bootstrap/testdata/bootstrap/99_openshift-machineconfig_master.yaml`

```yaml
apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
kind: MachineConfig
metadata:
  labels:
    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: master
  name: 99-master-ssh
spec:
  config:
    ignition:
      version: 3.2.0
    passwd:
      users:
      - name: core
        sshAuthorizedKeys:
        - ssh-rsa SSH-AUTHORIZED-KEY
    storage: {}
    systemd: {}
  osImageURL: ""
```

The worker equivalent is at `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/bootstrap/testdata/bootstrap/99_openshift-machineconfig_worker.yaml` with `role: worker` and `name: 99-worker-ssh`.

### Example 2: `storage.filesystems` with disk/raid (test template)

`/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/test/extended-priv/testdata/files/extra-disks.yaml`

### Example 3: From the SSH key update docs

`/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/Update-SSHKeys.md` (lines 31–48) shows the canonical minimal form:

```yaml
apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
kind: MachineConfig
metadata:
  labels:
    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
  name: 99-worker-ssh
spec:
  config:
    ignition:
      version: 2.2.0
    passwd:
      users:
      - name: core
        sshAuthorizedKeys:
        - ssh-rsa ABC123....
        - ssh-ed25519 XYZ7890....
```

Note the docs example still uses `2.2.0` — but any version the MCO accepts (2.2 through 3.5) will be converted internally to 3.5.0. For new configs, use `3.5.0`.

---

## 4. Ignition Spec Version Targeted by MCO

The MCO's internal canonical version is **3.5.0**, set as the constant:

`/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/common/constants.go`, line 110:

```go
InternalMCOIgnitionVersion = "3.5.0"
```

The comment (lines 105–109) explains the intent: all configs are converted to this version internally regardless of what version was submitted.

The `NewIgnConfig()` helper at `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/common/helpers.go` line 285 uses `ign3types.MaxVersion.String()`, which resolves to `3.5.0` from the vendor package at `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/vendor/github.com/coreos/ignition/v2/config/v3_5/types/config.go` (lines 27–30):

```go
MaxVersion = semver.Version{
    Major: 3,
    Minor: 5,
}
```

The ignition converter in `/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/pkg/controller/common/ignition_converter.go` supports bidirectional conversion between versions 2.2, 3.1, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, and 3.5. When users submit a config with `version: 3.2.0` (as seen in the bootstrap testdata YAMLs) or even `version: 2.2.0` (as seen in the docs), it is automatically converted to 3.5.0 for internal processing. For new MachineConfigs, you should specify `version: 3.5.0`.

| Field | In-place update supported? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `passwd.users[].sshAuthorizedKeys` (core only) | YES, no reboot | Only user `core`, only this field plus `passwordHash` |
| `passwd.users[].passwordHash` (core only) | YES, with reboot | Permitted by `verifyUserFields` alongside SSH keys |
| `passwd.users` — adding new users | NO | Rejected at reconcile: `"non-core user"` |
| `passwd.users` — other fields (HomeDir, Shell…) | NO | Rejected: `"SSH keys and password hash are not reconcilable"` |
| `passwd.groups` | NO | Rejected: `"ignition Passwd Groups section contains changes"` |
| `storage.filesystems` | NO (in-place) | Rejected: `"ignition filesystems section contains changes"` — only apply-on-new-node via `IrreconcilableValidationOverrides` |
| `storage.disks` | NO (in-place) | Same as filesystems |
| `storage.raid` | NO (in-place) | Same as filesystems |
| Ignition version targeted | **3.5.0** | All versions 2.2–3.5 accepted and converted |
